draft.introduction: The Orphic Rhapsodic Theogony (compiled c. 1st century BCE–2nd century CE) is a cosmogonic poem known from fragments quoted by Neoplatonist authors. It narrates divine successions culminating in the dismemberment of Dionysus by the Titans.

translation.original_ref: Orphic fragment 168K, preserved in Proclus, In Timaeum
translation.text: Zeus swallowed the firstborn king Phanes, and within himself he enclosed the bodies of all things. From within the sacred body of Zeus, all things emerged anew: the brilliant height of the broad sky, the sea, the noble earth, great Ocean, and the depths of Tartarus.
translation.original_text: Ζεὺς τότε πρωτογόνου βασιλέως κατεπίνυτο Φάνητα, τοῦ δὴ οἱ πάντων ἐντὸς αὐτοῦ σωμάτων ἐνεχώρει

translator_notes.note: Zeus’s swallowing of Phanes and re-creation of the cosmos from within himself is the Orphic version of pantheism: all things exist within the divine body. The Neoplatonists valued this passage highly, reading it as an allegory for the way the Demiurge contains the Forms.
